title: A vector as a point in space — music taste described as three numbers
verdict: PASS — all checks clear. Reading is 793 words (within 600–900), reads cleanly in ~3.5 min, all three citations present with [1] machinelearningmastery cited multiple times, no forward references taught, full faithfulness to corpus across 10 sampled assertions, beginner-accessible with concrete-first structure, well-formatted with no walls of text, no assessment references, and no scope creep.

format_discipline.notes: First non-empty line is H1 title. Sidecar reading.meta.json present and valid. Exactly 6 required sections in correct order. Diagram embedded at line 39 inside Key Concepts.

length.notes: 793 words is within [600, 900]. 793/230 = 3.45 min is within [3, 5].

citations.notes: [1] cited at lines 5, 11, 70, 90, 111. [2] cited at lines 54, 66, 76, 94. [3] cited at lines 19, 50, 95, 115. All markers resolve; no orphans.

deferred_terms_handled.text: Exact methods for measuring that distance, including tools like cosine similarity and distance metrics, are introduced in later topics.

forward_references.notes: dot_product, distance_formula, embeddings do not appear. cosine_similarity named but explicitly deferred, not taught. neighbourhood introduced and defined in corpus section 5.6 as in-scope vocabulary for 6.5; reading correctly includes it.

samples.assertion: every vector is also a location — a specific address in space
A coordinate is a single number that tells you how far to travel along one specific, named direction from a fixed starting point
2D vector [0.8, 0.6] plotted on Energy/Tempo axes step-by-step
Room table: Energy left-right, Tempo front-back, Mood up-down
No formula needed — conclusion from comparing point positions
Real AI systems work with vectors of 100, 512, or even thousands of features
Neighbourhood definition as set of points within small distance
Replicated the core logic of a recommendation engine without writing a single line of code

beginner_readability.notes: All new terms bold-defined at first use. Concrete analogies (city grid, room) precede abstraction. No hedging language. Average sentence length well under 25 words.

formatting.notes: No paragraph exceeds 6 sentences. Worked Example uses numbered list. In Practice uses bullet list. Inline examples appear in Key Concepts (city grid analogy, Alice/Bob/Carol comparison table). Bold-term/plain-meaning shape used consistently for all defined terms.

assessment_references.notes: No assessment IDs, due-week references, or grade weights appear anywhere in the reading.

scope.notes: All content (coordinate, axis, origin, coordinate_plane, point_in_space, distance_as_similarity, n_dimensional_space, taste_space, neighbourhood) is within topic scope. Cosine similarity and distance metrics correctly named-and-deferred at line 66. No teaching of dot_product, distance_formula, or embeddings.
